title: Non-Repudiation Evidence for Sensitive Transactions

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 AU-10 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AU-10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.9: Non-Repudiation Evidence for Sensitive Transactions.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.9' running on Application DB audit tables (via DBX), WinEventLog:Security logons, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Application DB audit tables (via DBX), `WinEventLog:Security` logons
app: Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742)
spl:
index=app sourcetype=app:txn_audit earliest=-24h status=success risk_tier="HIGH"
| eval join_key=md5(user.src_ip)
| join type=left join_key [
    search index=sec sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4624 earliest=-24h
    | eval join_key=md5(user.src_ip)
    | stats latest(_time) as last_logon by join_key, user, src_ip
  ]
| where isnull(last_logon)
| table _time, user, src_ip, txn_id, action
description: Chains application user actions to authentication events and source IPs to support AU-10 non-repudiation for high-risk transactions.
value: Chains application user actions to authentication events and source IPs to support AU-10 non-repudiation for high-risk transactions.
implementation: (1) Prefer `transaction` or `stats` over `join` at scale — prototype first; (2) hash PII keys consistently; (3) validate clock sync (AU-8); (4) document limitations where shared accounts break non-repudiation.
visualization: Table (orphaned high-risk txns), Timeline (txn vs logon), Stats (match rate)
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: Chains application user actions to authentication events and source to support AU-10 non-repudiation for high-risk transactions — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Application DB audit tables (via DBX), `WinEventLog:Security` logons.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Prefer `transaction` or `stats` over `join` at scale — prototype first; (2) hash PII keys consistently; (3) validate clock sync (AU-8); (4) document limitations where shared accounts break non-repudiation.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=app sourcetype=app:txn_audit earliest=-24h status=success risk_tier="HIGH"
| eval join_key=md5(user.src_ip)
| join type=left join_key [
    search index=sec sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4624 earliest=-24h
    | eval join_key=md5(user.src_ip)
    | stats latest(_time) as last_logon by join_key, user, src_ip
  ]
| where isnull(last_logon)
| table _time, user, src_ip, txn_id, action
```

Understanding this SPL

**Non-Repudiation Evidence for Sensitive Transactions (AU-10)** — Chains application user actions to authentication events and source IPs to support AU-10 non-repudiation for high-risk transactions.

Documented **Data sources**: Application DB audit tables (via DBX), `WinEventLog:Security` logons. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: app; **sourcetype**: app:txn_audit. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=app, sourcetype=app:txn_audit, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **join_key** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(last_logon)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Non-Repudiation Evidence for Sensitive Transactions (AU-10)**): table _time, user, src_ip, txn_id, action

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Non-Repudiation Evidence for Sensitive Transactions (AU-10)** — Chains application user actions to authentication events and source IPs to support AU-10 non-repudiation for high-risk transactions.

Documented **Data sources**: Application DB audit tables (via DBX), `WinEventLog:Security` logons. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (orphaned high-risk txns), Timeline (txn vs logon), Stats (match rate)
